For those who dont understand 

The Art and Industry: A Guide to the Filmmaking Process
​Filmmaking is often perceived as a singular, glamorous event centered on actors and directors, yet the reality is that creating a movie is a rigorous, multi-year process divided into five distinct and crucial phases. Moving a film from an initial concept to a final, polished product requires precise organization, immense collaboration, and a consistent vision. Understanding this structure—Development, Pre-Production, Production, Post-Production, and Distribution—is essential to grasping the true scope of cinematic art.
​The journey begins with Development and Pre-Production, the blueprint stages that define the film’s entire scope. Development involves concept generation, securing rights, and, most importantly, scriptwriting. Once the script is locked, Pre-Production commences. This phase is dedicated entirely to planning and scheduling, transforming the written word into a practical shooting strategy. Key tasks include hiring the core crew (cinematographer, production designer), casting the actors, location scouting, building sets, and meticulously budgeting. Every minute of the shoot is mapped out in the master document, often called the production schedule, which serves as the logistical bible for the entire project. The successful execution of a film is directly proportional to the thoroughness of its Pre-Production phase.
​The third stage, Production, is the execution phase, commonly referred to simply as "the shoot." Though often the shortest in duration, it is by far the most expensive and intense. The core objective is to capture all necessary visual and auditory material efficiently. On set, the director works closely with the Director of Photography (DP) to block scenes, manage lighting, and guide actor performances, while the sound mixer records clean dialogue. Maintaining continuity—ensuring elements like props, costumes, and actor positions match from one shot to the next—is paramount. Time is the most valuable commodity during Production, making meticulous planning from the earlier stages a necessity to avoid costly delays.
​Following the wrapping of the shoot, the project enters Post-Production, the stage where the raw footage is assembled into a coherent narrative. This is where the film truly finds its rhythm and emotional shape. The editor takes hundreds of hours of footage and crafts the structure, leading to the "picture lock" when no further changes to the visual timing are allowed. Simultaneously, sound designers create an immersive soundscape (adding ambient sounds, Foley effects, and ADR), and a composer writes the score. Finally, visual effects (VFX) are integrated, and the color grader adjusts every frame to create a consistent, dramatic mood, culminating in the finished master copy.
​The final phase is Distribution and Marketing, the complex process of connecting the completed film with an audience. This involves determining the release strategy, whether through major studios, independent distributors, or the film festival circuit. Marketing teams craft trailers, posters, and press materials designed to generate buzz and define the film's identity in the marketplace. For all the artistic effort expended in the prior phases, the success of the film as a commercial venture hinges on the effectiveness of its distribution plan. Filmmaking, therefore, is not merely a creative act but a massive industrial mobilization, demanding collaboration and adherence to a structured process to bring a shared vision to life.

If you like barbacoa, this recipe is awesome! I've made it three times, and it has been a hit with my family!

Chipotle Barbacoa

Ingredients: 

2-4 lbs. Chuck Roast
Salt & Pepper 
1 tbsp. each:  garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, chili powder, and oregano 
1 (7 oz) can chipotle sauce (not the chipotle peppers in sauce)
1 cup beef broth

Directions:

Season both sides of roast with salt and pepper; brown both sides. Transfer to a crock pot. Mix spices, chipotle sauce, and beef broth together; add to the roast. Cook on low for 8 hours. When meat is done, remove from crock pot, reserving the juice. Shred the meat and return it to the reserved juice.

Great for tacos, burritos, and other Mexican dishes.

This Is What Federal Government Violence Looks Like: The Killing of Renee Good

Do not call this a tragedy.
Do not call it a mistake.
Do not call it “complex.”

Renee Nicole Good was killed by the U.S. federal government.

She was shot dead by an ICE agent on a residential street, in daylight, in front of witnesses, for the unforgivable crime of not submitting quickly enough to armed federal authority. She was a citizen. She was unarmed. She was not threatening anyone. And she is dead because this government has decided that obedience matters more than life.

That is not law enforcement. That is federal government violence.

We are told—again—that the agent “feared for their life.” That phrase has become the most abused alibi in American governance. It is a get-out-of-murder card for anyone wearing a federal badge. If fear alone justifies killing, then the federal government has granted itself unlimited license to end civilian lives on the spot.

Fear is human. Killing is a choice.

Video evidence and eyewitness testimony show what ICE wants erased: chaos created by the agents themselves, a woman attempting to leave a dangerous situation, and a bullet fired through a windshield as a final, irreversible assertion of federal power. Renee Good’s wife said it best: “We had whistles. They had guns.” That sentence should haunt every official who dares defend this killing.

This administration wants the public to accept a new rule: any citizen who moves, hesitates, panics, or resists intimidation during a federal operation forfeits their right to live. That rule is not written in law books—but it is being enforced with bullets.

And when the killing was done, the cover-up began.

Federal authorities seized the investigation, locked out state officials, and buried evidence behind jurisdictional walls. Transparency was treated as a threat. Accountability as an inconvenience. This is not the behavior of a system confident in its righteousness; it is the behavior of an institution protecting itself.

ICE has become exactly what its critics warned it would become: a federal paramilitary force operating inside civilian neighborhoods with minimal oversight and maximum impunity. This is no longer about immigration enforcement. It is about control—about demonstrating who has the power to decide who lives and who dies within U.S. borders.

Renee Good did not die because of one rogue agent. She died because cruelty has been normalized, escalation rewarded, and because this administration has signaled—clearly—that lethal force will always be defended, no matter the cost in human life.

Let us be clear: this was cowardice. It takes no courage to fire a gun from behind a federal badge. It takes no bravery to kill a woman in a car. The real cowardice comes afterward—when officials hide, deflect, and sanitize a killing instead of confronting it.

People are in the streets because they recognize the truth: if this can happen to Renee Good, it can happen to anyone. If movement is a death sentence, if fear is justification, if investigation is optional, then citizenship itself becomes meaningless.

This is how democracies rot—not all at once, but bullet by bullet, excuse by excuse, badge by badge.

Justice for Renee Good does not mean a press release. It does not mean an internal review. It means real accountability, public transparency, and the dismantling of a federal enforcement culture that treats human beings as disposable.

We do not owe silence to federal power.
We do not owe patience to brutality.
We do not owe trust to agencies that kill and lie.

Say her name.
Demand the truth.
Refuse the lie that this was necessary.

Renee Good should be alive.
ICE and this administration should be held accountable.
They have no shame or deep reflection. 

James McGuire

Furthermore, as it specifies that I've the right to petition the Government for "redress of grievances", it follows that I've every right to air said "grievances", even in a very uncomplimentary manner.
